EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm with low pressure tracking out of the Gulf of Mexico to the Carolina coast along with an upper level low moving across the mountains brought periods of snow, sleet and freezing rain to both the mountains and piedmont with total snow amounts up to 8 inches. A significant number of power outages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and mixed precipitation totals ranged from 3 to 6 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying cyclone off the Southeast coast and cold air damming combined to produce heavy snow and occasional sleet along and near the eastern Blue Ridge escarpment. Rain mixed with sleet developed across the Blue Ridge mountains and North Carolina foothills during the evening, then changed to mainly sleet in most areas. Up to a quarter inch of sleet accumulated during the late evening and early morning hours. Precipitation then changed mostly to rain in most areas, before transitioning to snow during the pre-dawn hours of the 7th. As heavy snow continued to fall across the foothills and Blue Ridge mountains in North Carolina throughout the morning, heavy accumulations of snow became common. The region of heavy snowfall accumulation was confined to a very narrow corridor along the Blue Ridge south of I-40, but became more widespread across the northern mountains and foothills. Total snowfall accumulation generally ranged from 4-6 inches in these areas, with locally higher amounts reported in some high elevation locations near the Blue Ridge. This was in addition to the quarter inch or so of sleet that fell earlier in the morning. Meanwhile, accumulations were quite a bit lower in the areas of the northern mountains adjacent to the Tennessee border. The snow changed back to rain in most areas before ending late in the morning.

A slow moving low pressure system combined with weak cold air damming to produce an extended period of light freezing rain along the eastern Blue Ridge escarpment, as well as the northern foothills and Piedmont of North Carolina. Precipitation developed around daybreak of the 17th and continued to fall lightly for most of the day, occasionally mixing with sleet and snow. By evening, most areas reported between 0.10 and 0.20 inch of ice accretion, with a few locations reporting additional light snow/sleet accumulations. Sporadic power outages and multiple vehicle accidents were reported. Ice accretion dropped off considerably farther west toward the French Broad Valley in the southern/central Mountains and toward the Tennessee border in the northern mountains. The freezing rain tapered off to freezing drizzle during the evening of the 17th, but even this continued through the night in some areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain quickly changed over to snow before ending across central north Carolina as an arctic cold moved through the area. Snowfall amounts ranged from a dusting to as much as 2.5 inches across the Northern Piedmont and Northern Coastal Plain counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts averaged between 1 to 1.5 inches across the county. A glaze of ice from freezing rain was also reported on trees and power-lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms, with damaging winds, affected the area as a strong cold front moved through during peak heating. Observed winds around 60 mph resulted in numerous trees and power lines being blown down. There was one fatality in Chapel Hill due to a tree falling on a vehicle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees, power lines and power poles were blown down from near Chapel Hill to near Parkwood. A 55-year old man was killed by a falling tree as he was standing on the back deck of his home on Roper Lane in Chapel Hill, North Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant pressure rises behind a cold front resulted in strong non-convective winds ranging from 40 to 60 mph. The strong winds caused a tree to fall on a car, killing the driver and injuring the passenger.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 25 year old male was killed when a tree fell on his vehicle on the 2000 block of Reynolda Road. Also, a 26 year old female passenger sustained non life-threatening injuries.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of snow was reported across the county.

Sub-freezing air that gradually oozed south across the North Carolina Piedmont caused light rain to change to freezing rain during the early afternoon along the I-85 corridor. By late evening, many areas reported around 0.10 inch of ice accretion, with pockets of higher amounts. Most of the ice accretion was on elevated surfaces and warm roads prevented significant travel problems, although a few slick spots and accidents were reported. The freezing rain tapered off to freezing drizzle during the evening of the 17th, but even this continued through the night in some areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported flooding of Stallings Rd and Pharr Mill Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped southeast through eastern North Carolina during the evening of April 15th 2014. A band of showers and scattered thunderstorms developed just ahead of the front and a few storms produced strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust to 59 knots measured by sensor at Coast Guard Station in Hatteras.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds developed across the northern mountains late on the 29th, with the strongest winds affecting locations along and near the eastern escarpment of the Blue Ridge. The winds gusted to over 60 mph at times, with gusts in excess of 80 mph likely occurring across the higher elevations. Numerous trees were blown down in these areas. Sporadic minor damage was reported to roofs across the area and a downed power lines resulted in scattered power outages. A few trees fell on homes as well. The strong winds continued through much of the 30th before tapering off by mid-evening. Snow showers also developed within the northwest flow and produced 1-2 inches of accumulation during the overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front across western North Carolina during the afternoon. As the cluster pushed into the Piedmont, a significant downburst occurred on the north side of Statesville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported garage doors blown in and siding stripped off some homes along Scotts Creek Rd (4.5 NW) and multiple trees down on the northwest side of Statesville. Public reported minor roof damage to buildings near the city center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A classical cold air damning set up provided ample cold and dry air for central North Carolina. As low pressure tracked northeastward from the Gulf of Mexico to just off the Carolina coast, a major winter storm impacted the area. The precipitation started out as all snow across the entire area, but gradually transitioned to a snow/sleet mix and eventually mostly freezing rain across portions of the forecast area as a warm nose overspread portions of the region. Locations in the far northwestern portion of the forecast area stayed mostly all snow and received totals of 6-8 inches, with isolated amounts up to 10 inches. Snow amounts decreased further south and east as there was more mixed precipitation, but nevertheless everywhere saw at least 3-5 inches of snow/sleet. In addition, everywhere received at least a trace of freezing, with the Interstate 85 corridor receiving around a quarter inch of freezing rain. This resulted in some sporadic power outages and downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fall averaged 2-5 inches across the county. In addition, ice accrual ranged between a trace to 1/10 of an inch.

An intensifying cyclone off the Southeast coast and cold air damming combined to produce a period of heavy sleet followed by heavy icing across the northwest Piedmont of North Carolina. Sleet first developed during the late evening of the 6th, then began mixing with freezing rain around midnight. Sleet and freezing rain continued through the overnight into the daylight hours. Many areas reported around a half inch of sleet by around sunrise. Meanwhile, most areas changed completely to freezing rain around this time, which continued off and on through the morning, occasionally changing to or mixing with sleet or even snow. About a quarter inch of ice accumulated on top of the sleet, creating very dangerous driving conditions that resulted in many accidents. Power outages also became common during the morning, as trees, limbs, and power lines began to fall under the weight of the heavy ice in combination with winds gusting to around 30 mph.

Strong northwest winds developed across the northern mountains late on the 29th, with the strongest winds affecting locations along and near the eastern escarpment of the Blue Ridge. The winds gusted to over 60 mph at times, with gusts in excess of 80 mph likely occurring across the higher elevations. Numerous trees were blown down in these areas. Sporadic minor damage was reported to roofs across the area and a downed power lines resulted in scattered power outages. A few trees fell on homes as well. The strong winds continued through much of the 30th before tapering off by mid-evening. Snow showers also developed within the northwest flow and produced 1-2 inches of accumulation during the overnight hours.

As an upper air disturbance approached the region from the west, light to moderate snow developed across the North Carolina mountains around midnight early on the 25th, mainly in the high elevations of the southern mountains, and across much of the central and northern mountains (including the valleys) near the Tennessee border. The bulk of the snow accumulated during the morning of the 25th, when 2-4 inches fell across a fairly large area. Snow showers then redeveloped within northwest flow behind a cold front across the Tennessee border counties during the afternoon, resulting in additional accumulations, although those were primarily confined to the high elevations along the Tennessee border. Total accumulations ranged from 2-4 inches in the valleys across the Tennessee border counties (from I-40 north) and in the high elevations of the southern mountains, to 4-8 inches in the higher elevations along the Tennessee border.

In the midst of a late season cold air damming event, multiple waves of low pressure developed and tracked northeast along a stalled front just off the southeast coast. While some locations across the northwest Piedmont saw a dusting of snow, freezing rain was the main precipitation type. Freezing rain amounts averaged around a tenth of an inch, with an isolated quarter of an inch reported in Randolph County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accretion averaged around a tenth of an inch across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low deepening off the Carolina coast brought a wintry mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain to the northern-northwestern Piedmont counties. Snowfall amounts of 4 to 7 inches fell Forsyth, Person and Guilford counties. Just to the south and east of this area, a corridor of mainly sleet mixed with freezing rain produced significant icing of a quarter to half inch. This icing produced widespread downed trees and power outages over the northwest Piedmont. At the peak of the storm, over a 400,000 customers were without power. A natural disaster was declared in 7 |counties across the Raleigh CWA that were impacted by this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One quarter to one half of an inch of ice from freezing rain resulted in widespread downed trees and power-lines.  Additionally, snowfall of 1 to 2 inches fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low-level moisture surged northward ahead of a cold front and a weak surface wave. The moisture surge was enhanced by the passage of a sea breeze front. Afternoon and evening dewpoints reached the lower 60s. A weak mid-level shortwave helped showers to blossom into several thunderstorms which lingered for several hours after sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of nickels was reported at the intersection of Carver Drive and Summit Ridge Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm with low pressure tracking out of the Gulf of Mexico to the Carolina coast along with an upper level low moving across the mountains brought periods of snow, sleet and freezing rain to both the mountains and piedmont with total snow amounts up to 8 inches. A significant number of power outages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 7 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain quickly changed over to snow before ending across central north Carolina as an arctic cold moved through the area. Snowfall amounts ranged from a dusting to as much as 2.5 inches across the Northern Piedmont and Northern Coastal Plain counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of snow was reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds developed across the northern mountains late on the 29th, with the strongest winds affecting locations along and near the eastern escarpment of the Blue Ridge. The winds gusted to over 60 mph at times, with gusts in excess of 80 mph likely occurring across the higher elevations. Numerous trees were blown down in these areas. Sporadic minor damage was reported to roofs across the area and a downed power lines resulted in scattered power outages. A few trees fell on homes as well. The strong winds continued through much of the 30th before tapering off by mid-evening. Snow showers also developed within the northwest flow and produced 1-2 inches of accumulation during the overnight hours.

An intensifying cyclone off the Southeast coast and cold air damming combined to produce a period of heavy sleet followed by heavy icing across the northwest Piedmont of North Carolina. Sleet first developed during the late evening of the 6th, then began mixing with freezing rain around midnight. Sleet and freezing rain continued through the overnight into the daylight hours. Many areas reported around a half inch of sleet by around sunrise. Meanwhile, most areas changed completely to freezing rain around this time, which continued off and on through the morning, occasionally changing to or mixing with sleet or even snow. About a quarter inch of ice accumulated on top of the sleet, creating very dangerous driving conditions that resulted in many accidents. Power outages also became common during the morning, as trees, limbs, and power lines began to fall under the weight of the heavy ice in combination with winds gusting to around 30 mph.

Intensifying low pressure moving northward along the coast produced between one inch and four inches of snow across portions of northeast North Carolina from Wednesday afternoon, February 12th into Thursday morning, February 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally between one inch and three inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure moving northward along the coast produced between one inch and four inches of snow across portions of northeast North Carolina from Wednesday afternoon, February 12th into Thursday morning, February 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally between one half inch and one inch across the county. Belvidere (3 miles north-northeast) reported 1.0 inch of snow. Whiteston reported 1.0 inch of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper air disturbance approached the region from the west, light to moderate snow developed across the North Carolina mountains around midnight early on the 25th, mainly in the high elevations of the southern mountains, and across much of the central and northern mountains (including the valleys) near the Tennessee border. The bulk of the snow accumulated during the morning of the 25th, when 2-4 inches fell across a fairly large area. Snow showers then redeveloped within northwest flow behind a cold front across the Tennessee border counties during the afternoon, resulting in additional accumulations, although those were primarily confined to the high elevations along the Tennessee border. Total accumulations ranged from 2-4 inches in the valleys across the Tennessee border counties (from I-40 north) and in the high elevations of the southern mountains, to 4-8 inches in the higher elevations along the Tennessee border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying cyclone off the Southeast coast and cold air damming combined to produce a period of rain, sleet, and snow across portions of the southern and central North Carolina mountains as well as portions of the foothills. Precipitation began during the late evening of the 6th. Precipitation changed to rain in most areas after midnight, but not before 1-4 inches of snow had accumulated across the higher elevations of the Balsams, and across the Blue Ridge areas south of I-40. Sleet was favored across the foothills, and some locations saw enough to cover the ground. Meanwhile, rain changed briefly back to heavy snow around daybreak of the 7th, and a narrow corridor along the Blue Ridge areas of Henderson and Buncombe Counties saw 2-5 inch totals, while the lower elevations along the I-40 corridor saw 1-2 inches during this time.

Strong northwest winds developed across the mountains and portions of the foothills behind a cold front late on the 29th, with the strongest winds affecting locations along and near the eastern escarpment of the Blue Ridge. The winds gusted to over 60 mph at times (a peak gust of 62 mph was measured by the ASOS at the Asheville Regional Airport at around 730 AM). Gusts in excess of 80 mph likely occurred across the higher elevations. Numerous trees were blown down in these areas. Sporadic minor damage was reported to roofs across the area and a downed power lines resulted in scattered power outages, especially across the mid/upper French Broad Valley and surrounding locations. A few trees fell on homes as well. The strong winds continued through much of the 30th before tapering off by mid-evening.

An intensifying cyclone off the Southeast coast and cold air damming combined to produce heavy snow and occasional sleet along and near the eastern Blue Ridge escarpment. Rain mixed with sleet developed across the Blue Ridge mountains and North Carolina foothills during the evening, then changed to mainly sleet in most areas. Up to a quarter inch of sleet accumulated during the late evening and early morning hours. Precipitation then changed mostly to rain in most areas, before transitioning to snow during the pre-dawn hours of the 7th. As heavy snow continued to fall across the foothills and Blue Ridge mountains in North Carolina throughout the morning, heavy accumulations of snow became common. The region of heavy snowfall accumulation was confined to a very narrow corridor along the Blue Ridge south of I-40, but became more widespread across the northern mountains and foothills. Total snowfall accumulation generally ranged from 4-6 inches in these areas, with locally higher amounts reported in some high elevation locations near the Blue Ridge. This was in addition to the quarter inch or so of sleet that fell earlier in the morning. Meanwhile, accumulations were quite a bit lower in the areas of the northern mountains adjacent to the Tennessee border. The snow changed back to rain in most areas before ending late in the morning.

A line of showers and thunderstorms moved into the North Carolina Piedmont during the morning ahead of a strong cold front. An isolated storm within the line quickly and briefly became severe, producing a brief, weak tornado and subsequent downburst on the south and west side of Charlotte.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency managers' survey indicated a short tornado track on the south side of Charlotte. The tornado touched down at the end of Arrowpoint Blvd , where some siding was peeled off an industrial office building. The tornado tracked north/northeast along Arrowpoint Blvd, blowing down trees, tossing a large awning, |and tearing a glass panel from another industrial office building. The tornado then crossed Arrowood Rd within a half mile of I-77, where more than a dozen additional trees were blown down and the tops blown out of other trees. The tornado lifted in a wooded area just north/northeast of this point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms moved into the North Carolina Piedmont during the morning ahead of a strong cold front. An isolated storm within the line quickly and briefly became severe, producing a brief, weak tornado and subsequent downburst on the south and west side of Charlotte.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media and public reported sporadic tree damage across south and west Charlotte, as the tornadic thunderstorm moved north/northeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening line of showers and thunderstorms moved into the North Carolina mountains from East Tennessee during the morning. Localized damaging winds were observed along the leading edge, even after the line had begun to dissipate as it approached the Blue Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms reported numerous trees blown down near the high school.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure formed along a nearly stationary frontal boundary over northern Florida on February 11th, with a strong and large area of high pressure to the north.  Precipitation began as snow from the I-95 corridor and points west, with mainly sleet and freezing rain reported east of I-95.  The northwest portion of the region received over five inches of snow. Over the course of a couple of days, the storm transitioned to mainly an ice event, with nearly an inch of ice reported over interior portions of northeast South Carolina, with lesser amounts over southeast North Carolina.  In addition to creating extremely hazardous driving conditions, major power outages were reported due to downed limbs and power lines.  Low pressure finally moved up the eastern seaboard and out of the region during the day on February 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of light freezing rain and sleet began falling the morning of February 11th.  After a lull in the action, another round of light freezing rain fell on the 12th, and ended on the 13th.  Ice accumulations ranged from a half inch to three quarters of an inch. Driving conditions proved to be hazardous, and there were numerous power outages due to downed limbs and power lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emerald Isle Police Department reported dime to nickel size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure formed along a nearly stationary frontal boundary over northern Florida on February 11th, with a strong and large area of high pressure to the north.  Precipitation began as snow from the I-95 corridor and points west, with mainly sleet and freezing rain reported east of I-95.  The northwest portion of the region received over five inches of snow. Over the course of a couple of days, the storm transitioned to mainly an ice event, with nearly an inch of ice reported over interior portions of northeast South Carolina, with lesser amounts over southeast North Carolina.  In addition to creating extremely hazardous driving conditions, major power outages were reported due to downed limbs and power lines.  Low pressure finally moved up the eastern seaboard and out of the region during the day on February 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling the morning of February 11th, becoming heavy by early afternoon.  By that evening, over five inches had fallen over most of Bladen county, with 5.8 reported by an observer two miles ESE of Dublin.  The snow briefly transitioned to sleet and freezing rain with an additional quarter inch of ice accumulation before ending on February 13th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain began falling the morning of February 11th, with over a half inch of ice accumulation reported at the National Weather Service office in Wilmington.  Driving was treacherous, with numerous power outages reported.  The police department reported trees and branches down over much of Wilmington.  Some residents stayed overnight at emergency shelters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure tracking northeast across the central Appalachians combined with energy diving in from the northwest to result in a deep upper low that slowly moved east of the Blue Ridge to off the Mid-Atlantic coast. The system was preceded by shallow convective shower bands before the trailing strong cold front punched across the mountains in the early morning hours resulting in bursts of very strong north to northwest winds which topped 65 mph in spots along the southern Blue Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and large branches were brought down. The peak wind gust at Ashe County Airport (KGEV) was 53 mph at 535 EDT.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted over 50 mph throughout much of the day with a peak of 67 mph at 535 EST and several others of 60 mph or higher. One tree was reported down in Boone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds developed across the mountains and portions of the foothills behind a cold front late on the 29th, with the strongest winds affecting locations along and near the eastern escarpment of the Blue Ridge. The winds gusted to over 60 mph at times (a peak gust of 62 mph was measured by the ASOS at the Asheville Regional Airport at around 730 AM). Gusts in excess of 80 mph likely occurred across the higher elevations. Numerous trees were blown down in these areas. Sporadic minor damage was reported to roofs across the area and a downed power lines resulted in scattered power outages, especially across the mid/upper French Broad Valley and surrounding locations. A few trees fell on homes as well. The strong winds continued through much of the 30th before tapering off by mid-evening.

An intensifying cyclone off the Southeast coast and cold air damming combined to produce a period of rain, sleet, and snow across portions of the southern and central North Carolina mountains as well as portions of the foothills. Precipitation began during the late evening of the 6th. Precipitation changed to rain in most areas after midnight, but not before 1-4 inches of snow had accumulated across the higher elevations of the Balsams, and across the Blue Ridge areas south of I-40. Sleet was favored across the foothills, and some locations saw enough to cover the ground. Meanwhile, rain changed briefly back to heavy snow around daybreak of the 7th, and a narrow corridor along the Blue Ridge areas of Henderson and Buncombe Counties saw 2-5 inch totals, while the lower elevations along the I-40 corridor saw 1-2 inches during this time.

A strong surface low deepening off the Carolina coast brought a wintry mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain to the northern-northwestern Piedmont counties. Snowfall amounts of 4 to 7 inches fell Forsyth, Person and Guilford counties. Just to the south and east of this area, a corridor of mainly sleet mixed with freezing rain produced significant icing of a quarter to half inch. This icing produced widespread downed trees and power outages over the northwest Piedmont. At the peak of the storm, over a 400,000 customers were without power. A natural disaster was declared in 7 |counties across the Raleigh CWA that were impacted by this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 2 to 5 inches fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low-level moisture surged northward ahead of a cold front and a weak surface wave. The moisture surge was enhanced by the passage of a sea breeze front. Afternoon and evening dewpoints reached the lower 60s. A weak mid-level shortwave helped showers to blossom into several thunderstorms which lingered for several hours after sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters was reported on the Pender County side of the I-40 bridge. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying cyclone off the Southeast coast and cold air damming combined to produce heavy snow and occasional sleet along and near the eastern Blue Ridge escarpment. Rain mixed with sleet developed across the Blue Ridge mountains and North Carolina foothills during the evening, then changed to mainly sleet in most areas. Up to a quarter inch of sleet accumulated during the late evening and early morning hours. Precipitation then changed mostly to rain in most areas, before transitioning to snow during the pre-dawn hours of the 7th. As heavy snow continued to fall across the foothills and Blue Ridge mountains in North Carolina throughout the morning, heavy accumulations of snow became common. The region of heavy snowfall accumulation was confined to a very narrow corridor along the Blue Ridge south of I-40, but became more widespread across the northern mountains and foothills. Total snowfall accumulation generally ranged from 4-6 inches in these areas, with locally higher amounts reported in some high elevation locations near the Blue Ridge. This was in addition to the quarter inch or so of sleet that fell earlier in the morning. Meanwhile, accumulations were quite a bit lower in the areas of the northern mountains adjacent to the Tennessee border. The snow changed back to rain in most areas before ending late in the morning.
A line of showers and thunderstorms moved into the area along a strong cold front. There were several reports of wind damage as the line moved across the area during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms moved into the area along a strong cold front. There were several reports of wind damage as the line moved across the area during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team concluded a microburst impacted a very small section of single wide trailers along Crossbow Lane. The roofs were peeled back across a couple of the trailers and minor damage to the skirting was surveyed. Multiple trees were uprooted on the north side of Crossbow Lane. The path length was a tenth of a mile and the maximum path width was 200 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms moved into the area along a strong cold front early in the afternoon. The National Weather Service in Wilmington, North Carolina surveyed damage across Robeson County and determined there were two brief and distinct tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team concluded an EF-0 tornado with winds estimated to 70 mph touched down south of Elrod Road and west of O'Quinn Road and then moved north-northeast across a rural area. The tornado caused damage to a large shed that rolled over and it caused minor damage to a single wide home on the south side of Elrod Road. The tornado moved across Elrod Road and caused damage to three homes on the north side of the roadway. It lifted the porch off one of the homes and knocked down or snapped several trees as it moved into the woods behind the homes. The tornado lifted while in the woods on the north side of Elrod Road. The path length was just about a half mile and the maximum path width was 25 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms moved into the area along a strong cold front early in the afternoon. The National Weather Service in Wilmington, North Carolina surveyed damage across Robeson County and determined there were two brief and distinct tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team concluded an EF-0 tornado with winds estimated to 85 mph formed near the intersection of West Slyva Street and South Dean Street and moved north-northeast causing damage between South Dean Street and South Butler Street. Minor to moderate damage to homes in the vicinity of 3rd Avenue South was surveyed. Most of the homes suffered roof damage, but there were some lifted porches as well. Multiple sheds were destroyed. One large shed was lofted into a large oak tree about 40 yards from its original position. A large travel trailer was blown onto its side. Multiple trees were snapped and a couple of unanchored buildings were pushed off their foundation. The path length was 0.16 miles and the maximum path width was 35 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying center of low pressure passed north of the area and the trailing cold front was accompanied by very strong winds behind it as very cold air surged southward along with a large pressure rises.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were reports of trees down in several different locations in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain quickly changed over to snow before ending across central north Carolina as an arctic cold moved through the area. Snowfall amounts ranged from a dusting to as much as 2.5 inches across the Northern Piedmont and Northern Coastal Plain counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 1 inch across southern portions of county to as much as 2.0 inches across the north.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north through eastern North Carolina during the afternoon of April 7th 2014. In the wake of the front warm and unstable air spread north across the area. A small cluster of thunderstorms developed over eastern Beaufort County with one storm becoming severe and eventually producing a series of tornadoes. An EF2 tornado impacted parts of Beaufort County with an E1 over mainland Hyde County. There were no fatalities and 5 injuries associated with the tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado initially touched down near Highway 264 north of Belhaven. It began as an EF1 and tracked east-northeast toward Beech Ridge Road. Along Highway 264 there was damage to a few structures with numerous trees downed or snapped. One vehicle was lifted and tossed about 50 yards with 2 injuries. ||The tornado strengthened to a high end EF2 with winds estimated at 130 mph as it moved through the Beech Ridge Road area near Pantego. Several homes, out buildings and sheds were damaged or destroyed. A double wide mobile home was wiped off its foundation and destroyed despite being strapped down. Large numbers of trees were snapped, many at their base. ||The tornado then continued to produce EF2 damage on Ross Farm. A roof was blown off a house, an out building destroyed and large farm equipment moved. The tornado then dissipated in a field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north through eastern North Carolina during the afternoon of April 7th 2014. In the wake of the front warm and unstable air spread north across the area. A small cluster of thunderstorms developed over eastern Beaufort County with one storm becoming severe and eventually producing a series of tornadoes. An EF2 tornado impacted parts of Beaufort County with an E1 over mainland Hyde County. There were no fatalities and 5 injuries associated with the tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The second tornado produced from the same super-cell thunderstorm touched down near Ponzer. The tornado was rated as an EF1 with winds estimated at 105 mph. A home near Ponzer was pushed off its foundation but remained intact. Several trees were snapped or blow down. The tornado then tracked about a mile through rural areas before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system combined with weak cold air damming to produce an extended period of light freezing rain along the eastern Blue Ridge escarpment, as well as the northern foothills and Piedmont of North Carolina. Precipitation developed around daybreak of the 17th and continued to fall lightly for most of the day, occasionally mixing with sleet and snow. By evening, most areas reported between 0.10 and 0.20 inch of ice accretion, with a few locations reporting additional light snow/sleet accumulations. Sporadic power outages and multiple vehicle accidents were reported. Ice accretion dropped off considerably farther west toward the French Broad Valley in the southern/central Mountains and toward the Tennessee border in the northern mountains. The freezing rain tapered off to freezing drizzle during the evening of the 17th, but even this continued through the night in some areas.

Numerous showers and isolated thunderstorms crossed eastern North Carolina during the early morning hours of May 16th 2014 as a cold front approached from the west. One of the storms produced damaging winds across Lenoir County. Heavy rain led to some flooding in Beaufort County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds uprooted several trees. Two out buildings were shifted slightly off their foundations. Aluminum roof covering storage building was ripped off and ended up stuck in some trees.

This icing produced widespread downed trees and power outages over the northwest Piedmont. At the peak of the storm, over a 400,000 customers were without power. A natural disaster was declared in 7 |counties across the Raleigh CWA that were impacted by this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One quarter of an inch of ice from freezing rain resulted in widespread downed trees and power-lines.  Additionally, snowfall of 1 to 2 inches fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying cyclone off the Southeast coast and cold air damming combined to produce heavy snow and occasional sleet along and near the eastern Blue Ridge escarpment. Rain mixed with sleet developed across the Blue Ridge mountains and North Carolina foothills during the evening, then changed to mainly sleet in most areas. Up to a quarter inch of sleet accumulated during the late evening and early morning hours. Precipitation then changed mostly to rain in most areas, before transitioning to snow during the pre-dawn hours of the 7th. As heavy snow continued to fall across the foothills and Blue Ridge mountains in North Carolina throughout the morning, heavy accumulations of snow became common. The region of heavy snowfall accumulation was confined to a very narrow corridor along the Blue Ridge south of I-40, but became more widespread across the northern mountains and foothills. Total snowfall accumulation generally ranged from 4-6 inches in these areas, with locally higher amounts reported in some high elevation locations near the Blue Ridge. This was in addition to the quarter inch or so of sleet that fell earlier in the morning. Meanwhile, accumulations were quite a bit lower in the areas of the northern mountains adjacent to the Tennessee border. The snow changed back to rain in most areas before ending late in the morning.

A slow moving low pressure system combined with weak cold air damming to produce an extended period of light freezing rain along the eastern Blue Ridge escarpment, as well as the northern foothills and Piedmont of North Carolina. Precipitation developed around daybreak of the 17th and continued to fall lightly for most of the day, occasionally mixing with sleet and snow. By evening, most areas reported between 0.10 and 0.20 inch of ice accretion, with a few locations reporting additional light snow/sleet accumulations. Sporadic power outages and multiple vehicle accidents were reported. Ice accretion dropped off considerably farther west toward the French Broad Valley in the southern/central Mountains and toward the Tennessee border in the northern mountains. The freezing rain tapered off to freezing drizzle during the evening of the 17th, but even this continued through the night in some areas.

In the midst of a late season cold air damming event, multiple waves of low pressure developed and tracked northeast along a stalled front just off the southeast coast. While some locations across the northwest Piedmont saw a dusting of snow, freezing rain was the main precipitation type. Freezing rain amounts averaged around a tenth of an inch, with an isolated quarter of an inch reported in Randolph County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accretion averaged around two tenths of an inch across the county, with a tenth or two of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped southeast through eastern North Carolina during the evening of April 15th 2014. A band of showers and scattered thunderstorms developed just ahead of the front and a few storms produced strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered convection impacted central North Carolina as a deep upper level trough and associated strong surface cold front approached and crossed the area. Strong moisture transport resulted in above normal precipitable water values and this, combined with the ultimate training of the storms, lead to flash flooding as many areas received 2-4 inches of rain, with isolated amounts up to 5-6 inches. In addition, some isolated wind damage occurred and as a secondary area of low pressure developed and tracked along the front, an isolated tornado formed. This EF1 tornado uprooted numerous trees, causing substantial property damage near Durham, North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 55 near South Salem Street in Apex was closed due to high water. Observations indicated that 3-5 inches of rain fell throughout the county, with isolated amounts up to 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached eastern North Carolina during the afternoon of April 25th 2014. Warm southerly winds ahead of the front produced good instability over the region and numerous showers and thunderstorms crossed during the afternoon and evening. Two super-cell thunderstorms produced the bulk of the severe weather with multiple tornadoes reported in parts of Greene, Pitt and Beaufort counties. There was one indirect fatality as and elderly woman located near Washington died from pneumonia as a result of injuries sustained during the tornado. Total dollar damage was estimated at 15 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down at a farm along Beamon Old Creek Road producing EF1 damage with winds estimated at 110 mph. One side of a poultry farm was destroyed, moderate damage was also noted to outbuildings and two homes. The tornado then moved through farm and wooded areas before causing EF0 to low end EF1 damage to several mobile homes and one store near the intersection of Speights Bridge Road and Walston Road. The tornado then again moved over farm and wooded areas before dissipating near Howell Swamp Church Road. There were no injuries or fatalities associated with this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wedge cold front was stalled across southwest Virginia into north central North Carolina.  Temperatures behind the wedge front were well below seasonal normals with maximum temperatures only in the 50s.  Meanwhile...a very deep closed upper low (467mb at 500mb) was shifting slowing eastward from the central U.S. into the eastern U.S.  A deep conveyor belt of moisture was spreading northward from the Gulf of Mexico up and over the shallow wedge of cool air. This was a multi-day weather system that culminated with heavy rainfall, flooding, and flash flooding across the Virginia Piedmont during the afternoon of the 30th.  On this day, an early morning thunderstorm sparked a lightning-related fire in rural Surry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WXII Channel 12 Television of Winston-Salem, North Carolina reported that lightning struck and set on fire the detached carport of a home on Haystack Road in rural north central Surry county.  A vehicle inside the carport was also damaged.  There were no injuries or fatalities to report with the incident.  However, it took nearly two hours to contain the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered convection impacted central North Carolina as a deep upper level trough and associated strong surface cold front approached and crossed the area. Strong moisture transport resulted in above normal precipitable water values and this, combined with the ultimate training of the storms, lead to flash flooding as many areas received 2-4 inches of rain, with isolated amounts up to 5-6 inches. In addition, some isolated wind damage occurred and as a secondary area of low pressure developed and tracked along the front, an isolated tornado formed. This EF1 tornado uprooted numerous trees, causing substantial property damage near Durham, North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An overflowing pond washed out a section of West Cornwallis Road near the intersection of Owls Wood Lane. Observations indicated that 3-5 inches of rain fell throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure formed along a nearly stationary frontal boundary over northern Florida on February 11th, with a strong and large area of high pressure to the north.  Precipitation began as snow from the I-95 corridor and points west, with mainly sleet and freezing rain reported east of I-95.  The northwest portion of the region received over five inches of snow. Over the course of a couple of days, the storm transitioned to mainly an ice event, with nearly an inch of ice reported over interior portions of northeast South Carolina, with lesser amounts over southeast North Carolina.  In addition to creating extremely hazardous driving conditions, major power outages were reported due to downed limbs and power lines.  Low pressure finally moved up the eastern seaboard and out of the region during the day on February 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling the morning of February 11th, becoming heavy by early afternoon. By that evening, nearly five inches had fallen over most of Robeson county, with five inches reported by an amateur radio operator five miles SSW of Lumberton. The snow  transitioned freezing rain with a quarter inch of ice accumulation before ending the morning of  February 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Airmass became increasingly unstable as a warm front retreated northward during the morning hours, in advance of a compact but potent shortwave trough and associated surface cold front into the area from the west. Early in the event, discrete cells produced a weak EF-1 tornado touchdown in Halifax County along with severe hail ranging from quarter to golf ball size. As the storms progressed eastward into the coastal plain, they consolidated into a line and produced scattered wind damage before exiting the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power-lines were blown down at Silvertown Road and Norman Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached eastern North Carolina during the afternoon of April 25th 2014. Warm southerly winds ahead of the front produced good instability over the region and numerous showers and thunderstorms crossed during the afternoon and evening. Two super-cell thunderstorms produced the bulk of the severe weather with multiple tornadoes reported in parts of Greene, Pitt and Beaufort counties. There was one indirect fatality as and elderly woman located near Washington died from pneumonia as a result of injuries sustained during the tornado. Total dollar damage was estimated at 15 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous strong to severe thunderstorms moved into and developed over the North Carolina mountains during the evening ahead of an unusually strong mid-Summer cold front. While wind damage was the primary impact from the storms, a few instances of large hail were observed. One man was killed when a tree fell on his vehicle near Cherokee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee estimated up to 70 mph winds near downtown Asheville. Fire Dept reported multiple trees blown down from the city of Asheville south toward the Arden area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was up to the car windows on the Jackson Bypass near Garysburg. Several cars were stranded in flood waters. Numerous roads were flooded and impassable around Jackson.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 20 pines were snapped halfway up near NC Highway 43 where the tornado first touched down. At this point the tornado crossed through a heavily wooded area before crossing Ita road. In the vicinity of Ita road near Silvertown Road, there was significant damage to the roof of 2 mobile homes. A total of 8 mobile homes were damaged. In addition, 2 carports were destroyed and a steel rod driven through the wall of the most damaged mobile home. Two other houses sustained minor damage. Numerous trees were blown down along the path. The tornado continued for another 1/4 mile into a wooded area before lifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nighttime thunderstorms developed within an unstable air mass across the higher terrain of southwest North Carolina. Although several discrete cells exhibited strong rotation, only isolated large hail and isolated wind damage were reported. In addition, training thunderstorms resulted in locally heavy rainfall, with 2-4 falling in some areas in a short period of time. The combination of this heavy rainfall and a blocked culvert caused a significant flooding event in Woodfin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire Department reported that a little over 2 inches of rain that fell in around an hour combined with a blocked culvert to cause a small tributary of Beaverdam Creek to flood 6 homes to a depth of around 5 feet in the Brookdale Dr/Woodfin Ave area. Up to 30 people required rescue. The homes were heavily damaged by the flooding. Local COCORAHS reports showed storm total rainfall of 4 to 5 inches in this area, most of which fell in a fairly short period of time.

With the increased upper level forcing, fairly widespread convection developed across the area within the moderately unstable and moist air mass. Widespread trees were blown down, including several on homes and vehicles. Additionally, some large hail was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down along a swath from near Roxboro to several miles north of Surl.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous strong to severe thunderstorms moved into and developed over the North Carolina mountains during the evening ahead of an unusually strong mid-Summer cold front. Many of the storms exhibited characteristics more typical of organized storms seen in the Spring. While wind damage was the primary impact from the storms, a few instances of large hail were observed. One man was killed when a tree fell on his vehicle near Cherokee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a tree blown down near Lake Lure Dam (1.5 ESE Lake Lure) and another tree down on power lines near the intersection of Pheasant St and Redbird Dr (2.5 ESE). A spotter reported a tree blown down on a house in the Green Hill community, and trees blown down on Thunder Rd (2 S Rutherfordton).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon just north and south of a well-defined warm front bisecting central North Carolina. Three weak short-lived tornadoes developed along the warm front across eastern North Carolina. Training thunderstorms also resulted in some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads closed and impassable due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached eastern North Carolina during the afternoon of April 25th 2014. Warm southerly winds ahead of the front produced good instability over the region and numerous showers and thunderstorms crossed during the afternoon and evening. Two super-cell thunderstorms produced the bulk of the severe weather with multiple tornadoes reported in parts of Greene, Pitt and Beaufort counties. There was one indirect fatality as and elderly woman located near Washington died from pneumonia as a result of injuries sustained during the tornado. Total dollar damage was estimated at 15 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree blown down onto mobile home.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter observed a tornado briefly touch down about 5 miles west of Greenville. There was no observed damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Airmass became increasingly unstable as a warm front retreated northward during the morning hours, in advance of a compact but potent shortwave trough and associated surface cold front into the area from the west. Early in the event, discrete cells produced a weak EF-1 tornado touchdown in Halifax County along with severe hail ranging from quarter to golf ball size. As the storms progressed eastward into the coastal plain, they consolidated into a line and produced scattered wind damage before exiting the forecast area.

Scattered thunderstorms developed over the high terrain throughout the afternoon and moved east, while additional numerous thunderstorms moved into western North Carolina from East Tennessee during the late afternoon and early evening. Multiple localized severe thunderstorms developed from this activity and produced quite a bit of large hail along with isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: FD reported quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed over the mountains and foothills during early-to-mid afternoon and moved into the Piedmont by late afternoon and early evening. A few storms produced large hail and/or damaging winds, mainly over the foothills and Piedmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reported nickel size hail on I-40 near mile marker 116.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed over the mountains and foothills during early-to-mid afternoon and moved into the Piedmont by late afternoon and early evening. A few storms produced large hail and/or damaging winds, mainly over the foothills and Piedmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reported nickel size hail on Cape Hickory Rd (3 ENE Icard) and on West View Ave near the Hickory Airport (4 ENE).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed over the mountains and foothills during early-to-mid afternoon and moved into the Piedmont by late afternoon and early evening. A few storms produced large hail and/or damaging winds, mainly over the foothills and Piedmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: FD reported two trees blown down on a house and trees down on power lines south of Lincolnton. Also, Ham radio operator reported at least two trees blown down long Highway 321 in this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to widely scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon across the mountains, then formed into several small clusters as they moved across the foothills and Piedmont from mid-afternoon into early evening. Isolated wind damage and localized, marginally severe hail occurred with some of this activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 3/4 inch size hail fell between Etowah and Horse Shoe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed over eastern North Carolina during the afternoon and early evening of June 5th. Several of the storms became severe producing damaging winds and large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed over eastern North Carolina during the afternoon and early evening of June 5th. Several of the storms became severe producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew down trees and power lines between Blounts Creek and Cox Crossroads. One minor injury was reported from a falling tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed over eastern North Carolina during the afternoon and early evening of June 5th. Several of the storms became severe producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported trees blown down by strong winds near Bayboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed over eastern North Carolina during the afternoon and early evening of June 5th. Several of the storms became severe producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew down a tree along Catherine Lake Road to the west of Richlands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and an attendant cold front crossed the Mid-Atlantic Region on April 25, producing severe wind gusts and four tornadoes, including two long tracked EF-2 tornadoes over northeast North Carolina during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado started near Blackrock in extreme eastern Bertie county, then tracked east-northeast across the Chowan River. Trees were downed or snapped off and power lines were downed. The tornado then tracked into Chowan county from Bertie county.  Damage in much of the tornado path was EF-0 to weak EF-1 in intensity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and an attendant cold front crossed the Mid-Atlantic Region on April 25, producing severe wind gusts and four tornadoes, including two long tracked EF-2 tornadoes over northeast North Carolina during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado tracked from the Chowan River east-northeast through Chowan county into the Bear Swamp at the Chowan/Perquimans county line. Trees were downed or snapped off and power lines were downed near Macedonia Road and Chambers Ferry Road. The tornado then continued northeast into Perquimans county from Chowan county. Damage in much of the tornado path was EF-0 to EF-1 in intensity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and an attendant cold front crossed the Mid-Atlantic Region on April 25, producing severe wind gusts and four tornadoes, including two long tracked EF-2 tornadoes over northeast North Carolina during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado continued from near Bear Swamp at the Chowan/Perquimans line east-northeast to just east of Chapanoke near the Perquimans/Pasquotank line. Numerous trees were downed or snapped off and power lines were downed. Several farm buildings were damaged or destroyed. Numerous homes were damaged or destroyed in the Chapanoke area. The tornado then moved northeast into Pasquotank county from Perquimans county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and an attendant cold front crossed the Mid-Atlantic Region on April 25, producing severe wind gusts and four tornadoes, including two long tracked EF-2 tornadoes over northeast North Carolina during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF-1 tornado occurred just south of another tornado which tracked from near Nixonton to just south southeast of Elizabeth City. The tornado path included a section of Nixonton Road and Body Road. Numerous trees were downed and a few mobile homes were damaged on Lobell Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and an attendant cold front crossed the Mid-Atlantic Region on April 25, producing severe wind gusts and four tornadoes, including two long tracked EF-2 tornadoes over northeast North Carolina during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado tracked from just southeast of Chapanoke at the Perquimans/Pasquotank line northeast to just northeast of Elizabeth City where it dissipated. Several homes or mobile homes were damaged or destroyed on Foreman Bundy Road and Lobell Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and an attendant cold front crossed the Mid-Atlantic Region on April 25, producing severe wind gusts and four tornadoes, including two long tracked EF-2 tornadoes over northeast North Carolina during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado continued from near Burnt Mill Road near the Chowan/Perquimans line northeast to Woodville near the Perquimans/Pasquotank line. Trees and power lines were downed just southwest of Bethel. Several houses were damaged or destroyed and power lines were downed on Snug Harbor Road. Trees were also downed on several homes. The tornado then tracked northeast into Pasquotank county from Perquimans county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and isolated thunderstorms crossed eastern North Carolina during the early morning hours of May 16th 2014 as a cold front approached from the west. One of the storms produced damaging winds across Lenoir County. Heavy rain led to some flooding in Beaufort County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain led to water covering roads in downtown Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong thunderstorms develop just north of a stalled front over parts of eastern North Carolina during the evening of May 23rd 2014. One of the storms produced hail up to the size of a nickel in Onslow County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front approached from the north during the late afternoon and evening. Thunderstorms developed late in the afternoon and quickly increased in coverage. Some of the thunderstorms did produce severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reportedly broke a window on a mobile home on John Pickett Road. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unstable air was situated over the region south of a warm front and east of an approaching cold front. Showers and storms developed during the late afternoon and continued into the nighttime. A couple of storms increased to severe levels and produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew two trees down along North 7th Street including one near Roach Street and one near West Jackson Street. Damage values are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a tree down near the interchange of Highway 29 and Business Highway 29. Damage values are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage consisted of dozens of snapped and uprooted trees and nearly a dozen homes that experienced roof damage. The roof damage ranged from loss of shingles, to one single-wide home that lost nearly its entire roof. The extent of damage to the single-wide home along with the nearby tree damage is consistent with maximum wind speeds of 90-95 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado continued from Woodville near the Perquimans/Pasquotank line northeast across Pasquotank county into the Pasquotank River just east of the Elizabeth City Airport. Numerous trees were downed or snapped off and power lines were downed. Some homes were damaged. The tornado started to weaken a bit as it moved into Camden county from Pasquotank county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong instability and some frontal lift combined to produce pulse severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of quarters was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong instability and some frontal lift combined to produce pulse severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was reported down and blocking Locklear Road. The fire department was called to remove the tree. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening line of showers and thunderstorms moved into the North Carolina mountains from East Tennessee during the morning. Localized damaging winds were observed along the leading edge, even after the line had begun to dissipate as it approached the Blue Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reported multiple trees blown down in the Riceville Community (4 E Asheville), with some trees on homes and one tree falling on a moving vehicle on Lower Grassy Branch Rd. Spotter also reported numerous trees down west of Black|Mountain. The wind damage was the result of outflow from a dissipating line of thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached eastern North Carolina during the afternoon of April 25th 2014. Warm southerly winds ahead of the front produced good instability over the region and numerous showers and thunderstorms crossed during the afternoon and evening. Two super-cell thunderstorms produced the bulk of the severe weather with multiple tornadoes reported in parts of Greene, Pitt and Beaufort counties. There was one indirect fatality as and elderly woman located near Washington died from pneumonia as a result of injuries sustained during the tornado. Total dollar damage was estimated at 15 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down approximately 3 miles north of Shine near the intersection of Shine Road and Kearney Cemetery Road. The tornado was rated as an EF0 with winds estimated at 80 mph. Six homes were impacted with damage to mainly siding and porches. One mobile home was severely damaged from a tree falling on it. An amateur radio antenna was also bent in half. there were no injuries or fatalities associated with this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached eastern North Carolina during the afternoon of April 25th 2014. Warm southerly winds ahead of the front produced good instability over the region and numerous showers and thunderstorms crossed during the afternoon and evening. Two super-cell thunderstorms produced the bulk of the severe weather with multiple tornadoes reported in parts of Greene, Pitt and Beaufort counties. There was one indirect fatality as and elderly woman located near Washington died from pneumonia as a result of injuries sustained during the tornado. Total dollar damage was estimated at 15 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down approximately one mile northeast of Chicod causing EF1 damage to hard and softwood trees. The tornado then caused EF1 to low end EF2 damage with winds estimated to 115 mph as it hit several mobile homes, some of which were destroyed. Farm equipment and outbuildings were damaged along Blackjack-Simpson Road. A tractor trailer was flipped as well before the tornado dissipated across some farm fields. There were no injuries reported with this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon just north and south of a well-defined warm front bisecting central North Carolina. Three weak short-lived tornadoes developed along the warm front across eastern North Carolina. Training thunderstorms also resulted in some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado first touched down along State Road 1526 on the south side of highway 64 where numerous trees were downed or snapped. Intermittent damage continued along state road 1526 on the north side of Highway 64 where a large tree was uprooted and several outbuildings experienced roof damage. Intermittent tree damage continued northeast across State Road 1527. Numerous single wide homes and outbuildings received minor damage as the tornado continued northeast across Mooring Road. The tornado lifted at Highway 111 just east of the intersection with Mooring Road, where several single wide homes received minor damage and numerous trees were downed or snapped. The intermittent nature of damage indicates that the tornado may have briefly lifted a few times along the 4 mile path. The damage observed is consistent with maximum wind speeds of 70 to 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture and a stalled front near the coast led to numerous showers and a few thunderstorms across eastern North Carolina during the afternoon of July 21st. Very heavy rain led to localized flooding across Carteret County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of 3 to 5 inches produced flooding across much of Beaufort. The water was up to two feet deep on some roads with cars stalled in the flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across eastern North Carolina during the evening of June 13th as an upper level disturbance crossed the region. Some of the storms produced very heavy rain and small hail with flooding observed in parts of Beaufort County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beaufort County 911 reported a funnel cloud near River Road just southeast of Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across eastern North Carolina during the evening of June 13th as an upper level disturbance crossed the region. Some of the storms produced very heavy rain and small hail with flooding observed in parts of Beaufort County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent showers and thunderstorms led to rainfall amounts over 3 inches across central Beaufort County. Emergency Manager reported several roads in the city of Washington were flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching upper level disturbance produced scattered thunderstorms across eastern North Carolina during the early afternoon of July 27th. An isolated storm became severe producing damaging winds across Greene County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strongs blew the siding of a building along third street in Snow Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching upper level disturbance produced scattered thunderstorms across eastern North Carolina during the early afternoon of July 27th. An isolated storm became severe producing damaging winds across Greene County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew the roof of a house on Nahunta Road in Snow Hill.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported the first of two severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds that blew down a few trees just north of Maggie Valley. Sporadic damage continued east to the Lake Junaluska (tree down on County Rd) and Waynesville and Canton areas, where trees were blown down on Ratcliff Rd (3 ENE Waynesville), Chambers Farm Ln (6 SSW Canton) and on Highway 276 (6 S Canton).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed over eastern North Carolina during the afternoon and early evening of June 5th. Several of the storms became severe producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cherry Point ASOS reported a wind gust of 54 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary lingered for another day triggering thunderstorms during the afternoon hours across extreme southwest North Carolina. A few of the storms downed some trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway department personnel reported several trees downed by thunderstorm wind 7 miles east-northeast of Hayesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front tracked into the afternoon hours across extreme southwest North Carolina.  Thunderstorms developed with many trees were downed by the thunderstorm wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement personnel reported several trees were downed by thunderstorm wind in Murphy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached eastern North Carolina during the afternoon of April 25th 2014. Warm southerly winds ahead of the front produced good instability over the region and numerous showers and thunderstorms crossed during the afternoon and evening. Two super-cell thunderstorms produced the bulk of the severe weather with multiple tornadoes reported in parts of Greene, Pitt and Beaufort counties. There was one indirect fatality as and elderly woman located near Washington died from pneumonia as a result of injuries sustained during the tornado. Total dollar damage was estimated at 15 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon just north and south of a well-defined warm front bisecting central North Carolina. Three weak short-lived tornadoes developed along the warm front across eastern North Carolina. Training thunderstorms also resulted in some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This short track tornado was on the ground for a very short time, but during this time it completely leveled a hog house and snapped and downed numerous trees. Another near-by home experienced roof damage. The extent of damage to the hog house suggests maximum wind speeds of 95-100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level low west of the area provided support for numerous showers and thunderstorms. There was a report of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree reportedly fell on a car. The fire department was dispatched.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong instability and convergent low-level flow along and south of a stalled front helped initiate a cluster of severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail stones up to 1.75 inches were measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong instability and convergent low-level flow along and south of a stalled front helped initiate a cluster of severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of golf balls was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Airmass became increasingly unstable as a warm front retreated northward during the morning hours, in advance of a compact but potent shortwave trough and associated surface cold front into the area from the west. Early in the event, discrete cells produced a weak EF-1 tornado touchdown in Halifax County along with severe hail ranging from quarter to golf ball size. As the storms progressed eastward into the coastal plain, they consolidated into a line and produced scattered wind damage before exiting the forecast area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power-lines were blown near the intersection of Galatia Church Road and Will Monroe Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous strong to severe thunderstorms moved into and developed over the North Carolina mountains during the evening ahead of an unusually strong mid-Summer cold front. Many of the storms exhibited characteristics more typical of organized storms seen in the Spring. While wind damage was the primary impact from the storms, a few instances of large hail were observed. One man was killed when a tree fell on his vehicle near Cherokee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported a 53-year-old man was extricated from his vehicle then later died from injuries suffered after a tree fell on his vehicle as he was traveling along Old Number 4/Jess Nations Rd. Other trees were reported down in the area as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and an attendant cold front crossed the Mid-Atlantic Region on April 25, producing severe wind gusts and four tornadoes, including two long tracked EF-2 tornadoes over northeast North Carolina during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado started just south of Edenton and tracked northeast to near Burnt Mill Road near the Chowan/Perquimans county line. Numerous trees were downed or snapped off. Houses were damaged or destroyed on Yeopim Road. An 11-month-old boy died two days after being injured during the tornado. One injury was also reported. The tornado then tracked northeast into Perquimans county from Chowan county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado continued from the Pasquotank River northeast into Camden county where it dissipated just east of Indiantown. Numerous trees were downed or snapped off and power lines were downed. There was some structural damage.

Large clusters of thunderstorms erupted in the warm and humid air-mass ahead of a surface cold front and upper trough. A few of these thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts along with prolific lightning and persistent heavy downpours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The media relayed a viewer report of trees down on Furnie Hammond Road. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a upper level trough finally began to shift east, height falls aloft overspread the region, in addition several upper level disturbances affected the area. With the increased upper level forcing, fairly widespread convection developed across the area within the moderately unstable and moist air mass. Widespread trees were blown down, including several on homes and vehicles. Additionally, some large hail was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were blown down along a swath from several miles southwest of Clayton to several miles southwest of Princeton. In addition, crops were damaged near Princeton, as well as a few small barns. Also, shingles were blown off of a couple of homes. Monetary damages were unknown and were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed in the unstable, moisture laden air in advance of an approaching strong cold front. A couple of these storms reached severe levels by producing damaging winds and quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across the region during the day. Showers and storms developed within the unstable air to its south by the afternoon. Some of these storms increased to severe levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew three trees down in the Allison Road vicinity of Quick. Damage values are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down between 10 and 15 trees in the vicinity of Quick. Most of the trees were blown down near the intersection of Allison Road and Highway 158. The remainder of the trees were along Highway 158 towards Casville. Damage valuess are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across the region during the day. Showers and storms developed within the unstable air to its south by the afternoon. Some of these storms increased to severe levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house and damaged multiple electronic devices. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid conditions lead to the development of showers and storms during the afternoon heating period of the day. Some of these storms increased to severe levels and produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew two trees down southwest of East Bend. Powerlines were brought down by one of the falling trees, and one of the trees came to rest over Indian Heaps Road. Damage values are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down trees and large limbs. Damage values are estimated.
