EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms were able to organize ahead of a cold front with one storm becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A team from the National Weather Service conducted a survey and found that a poorly constructed single wide trailer had three-quarters of its roof removed. Two people were inside the home at the time the damage occurred, but were not injured.There was a snapped pine tree and a snapped rotted hardwood tree nearby. A few ornamental trees, some of which were also rotted were snapped along Baltimore Road. Power lines were also reported down. The damage pattern was consistent with straight line winds up to 60 mph. Time of event was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developing over the mountains during the late afternoon organized into a broken line, which moved across the foothills and Piedmont during the evening. A few brief severe storms occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Newspaper reported multiple trees and large limbs blown down across Gaston County, particularly in the Dallas and Lowell areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid conditions lead to the development of showers and storms during the afternoon heating period of the day. Some of these storms increased to severe levels and produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell on Somers Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to widely scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon across the mountains, then formed into several small clusters as they moved across the foothills and Piedmont from mid-afternoon into early evening. Isolated wind damage and localized, marginally severe hail occurred with some of this activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reported numerous trees and power lines blown down on the north side of Morganton, especially on Putnam St and a tree through a mobile home on Amherst Road (2 NE Morganton) with people briefly trapped inside. Trees were also down in Drexel, along Seitz Rd and Falls Dr just (2-3 miles north/ northwest of Valdese) and on Mineral Springs Road (1.5 S Valdese). Another tree was down on a house on Connelly Springs Church Rd (0.5 S Connelly Springs).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid conditions lead to the development of showers and storms during the afternoon heating period of the day. Some of these storms increased to severe levels and produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several large tree branches on Somers Road. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was located near the crest of the blue ridge. Daytime heating and wind convergence along the higher terrain helped develop some showers and storms during the mid to late afternoon hours. One storm reached severe levels with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down a set of powerlines in the community of Hays. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed near the Blue Ridge during early-to-mid afternoon, then organized into a cluster of showers and storms as they moved into the Piedmont during the late afternoon, where isolated, brief damaging winds occurred. The cluster of storms slowed and briefly stalled over southern portions of the Charlotte metro area, resulting in significant flash flooding near the Mecklenburg/Union County line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding developed along the Goose Creek basin when 3.5 to 5 inches of rain fell in less than two hours near the Union/Mecklenburg County line. A stream gauge on Stevens Mill Rd rose above the established flood stage around 630 pm EDT and crested more than 2 feet above flood stage shortly after 7 pm, flooding a portion of Stevens Mill Rd. The water moved rapidly downstream, with a stream gauge at Mill Grove Rd exceeding established flood stage shortly after 7 pm, also cresting more than 2 feet above flood stage around 830 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms, with a few severe storms developed over the Piedmont late in the afternoon and during the early evening. Meanwhile, a weakening complex of thunderstorms moved into the Smoky Mountain region, producing areas of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Newspaper reported a large tree fell on and destroyed a workshop building in Glen Alpine. NWS employee reported multiple large limbs blown down in the area around Broughton Hospital (1 SSE Morganton). Public reported trees blown down off Bethel Rd (2 ESE) and numerous trees blown down just northeast of town. Spotter reported a tree blown down on a house at North Anderson St and Collett St (where two children were briefly trapped) in the city, and another tree on a house off Highway 70 between Morganton and Drexel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance passed along an approaching cold front. Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across the region. Some of these thunderstorms increased to severe levels and produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a tree down on Highway 8. Damage values are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew one tree down on Welch Street. Damage values are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell at the Mayberry Mall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew down a tree near Willow Street in Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across eastern North Carolina during the evening of June 19th as a cold front approached from the north. A few of the storms became severe producing damaging winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew down a tree near Highway 264 and Cherry Lane near Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another day of hot and humid conditions and unstable air helped to generate some isolated showers and storms. One of these storms increased to severe levels and produced damaging winds near King in Stokes County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds produced scattered damage over about a two mile region of Stokes County, near the community of King. Around 6 to 8 trees were blown down at Old Route 52 and Chestnut Grove Road. Thunderstorm winds caused siding damage to houses and downed trees and limbs along Sterling Pointe Road. On Carson Watts road, a tree was blown down onto powerlines. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a building in Fairmont, causing significant damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the Lumber River Electric Company building at the corner of Main and Red Cross Streets. The resulting fire destroyed the interior of the structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance passed along an approaching cold front. Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across the region. Some of these thunderstorms increased to severe levels and produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a tree down on YMCA Camp Road. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance passed along an approaching cold front. Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across the region. Some of these thunderstorms increased to severe levels and produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a power line down at the intersection of Brushy Mountain Road and Country Club Road. Damage values are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree blown down by strong winds near Turkey Trot Road east of Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed near the Blue Ridge during early-to-mid afternoon, then organized into a cluster of showers and storms as they moved into the Piedmont during the late afternoon, where isolated, brief damaging winds occurred. The cluster of storms slowed and briefly stalled over southern portions of the Charlotte metro area, resulting in significant flash flooding near the Mecklenburg/Union County line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although heavy rainfall ended across northern Union County by 9 pm, flooding continued in the Goose Creek basin, as excessive runoff continued to work its way downstream, with a stream gauge near Fairview eventually exceeding the established flood stage after 1 AM on the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The outer rain bands associated with Hurricane Arthur produced locally heavy rainfall of 3 to 4 inches and minor flooding across Edgecombe County. Additionally, an area of convection which developed well west of the circulation produced some isolated thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water was reported on Cutchin Farm Road. Division of Highways were dispatched to put up signs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pine tree was snapped off at Hilltop Avenue and Circle Drive. An oak tree in Cary was also snapped in half.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developing across the Tennessee Valley ahead of a cold front moved into the northern mountains of North Carolina during the evening. One isolated storm produced large hail in Mitchell County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developing across the Tennessee Valley ahead of a cold front moved into the northern mountains of North Carolina during the evening. One isolated storm produced large hail in Mitchell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reported 3/4 inch hail north of the Oak Hill community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms, with a few severe storms developed over the Piedmont late in the afternoon and during the early evening. Meanwhile, a weakening complex of thunderstorms moved into the Smoky Mountain region, producing areas of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported up to penny size hail on the west side of Gastonia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon showers and thunderstorms developing across the mountains formed into a broken line that moved across the foothills and Piedmont during the late afternoon and evening. A few of the storms became briefly severe, producing localized downbursts. Locally heavy rainfall and urban flooding was reported in the Charlotte metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: FD and public reported flash flooding throughout the west side of Charlotte, primarily as a result of poor drainage, after 2-3 inches of rain fell in just a couple of hours. Multiple roads were closed throughout this area, including Freedom Drive, which was reported to have as much as two feet of water over it just northwest of downtown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed near the Blue Ridge during early-to-mid afternoon, then organized into a cluster of showers and storms as they moved into the Piedmont during the late afternoon, where isolated, brief damaging winds occurred. The cluster of storms slowed and briefly stalled over southern portions of the Charlotte metro area, resulting in significant flash flooding near the Mecklenburg/Union County line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cluster of slow moving and repeating thunderstorms produced 3.5 to 5 inches of rain in less than two hours near the Mecklenburg/ Union County line. Severe urban and small stream flooding occurred in the Matthews area, with water up to the windows of some vehicles. Stream flooding included a tributary of McAlpine Creek which flooded a part of Sam Newell Rd. Multiple roads were closed throughout the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a upper level trough finally began to shift east, height falls aloft overspread the region, in addition several upper level disturbances affected the area. With the increased upper level forcing, fairly widespread convection developed across the area within the moderately unstable and moist air mass. Widespread trees were blown down, including several on homes and vehicles. Additionally, some large hail was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down along a swath from several miles west of Durham to near Bethesda. In addition, 2 homes had trees fall on them. Monetary damages were unknown and were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed in the unstable, moisture laden air in advance of an approaching strong cold front. A couple of these storms reached severe levels by producing damaging winds and quarter size hail.

Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed in the unstable, moisture laden air in advance of an approaching strong cold front. A couple of these storms reached severe levels by producing damaging winds and quarter size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees within an area north of Reidsville. Specific locations include Wolf Island Road and Mount Carmel Church Road. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of the most significant winter storm of the season, a lead shortwave induced cyclongensis along a stalled surface boundary located across central South Carolina and Georgia. As the low tracked northeastward, snow spread across southeastern North Carolina. Three to four inches ended up falling across Scotland, Hoke, Cumberland, Sampson and Wayne counties. Amounts quickly dropped off further north and west, with a trace to 2-3 inches falling as far north as the US Highway 64 corridor and nothing elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow averaged just a trace across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure tracking northeast across the central Appalachians combined with energy diving in from the northwest to result in a deep upper low that slowly moved east of the Blue Ridge to off the Mid-Atlantic coast. The system was preceded by shallow convective shower bands before the trailing strong cold front punched across the mountains in the early morning hours resulting in bursts of very strong north to northwest winds which topped 65 mph in spots along the southern Blue Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sporadic tree damage was reported mainly across northern sections of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air trough combined with deep moisture source to produce heavy snow over southwest  North Carolina.  The heaviest snow blanketed the highest elevations where up to 7.5 inches was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observation reported 7.5 of snow fell 15.5 miles west-northwest of Murphy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front advanced through the area. Ahead of it isolated thunderstorms developed which contained damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging thunderstorm winds downed trees in the southwest part of King. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold high pressure was in place across the region on Tuesday, February 11, 2014. An area of low pressure progressed northward from the Gulf of Mexico the 11th into the 12th of February and transported a generous amount of moisture into and across the cold air over the area allowing the precipitation to fall as moderate to heavy snow. During Tuesday night into Wednesday the low passed over the region and brought some air just slightly above freezing into the lower portions of the atmosphere. This allowed for the snow to transition into a period of freezing rain and/or sleet in areas east of the foothills. However, during the day on Thursday, February 13, 2014, the low pressure progressed northeast of the region, and sub-freezing air in the lower parts of the atmosphere returned and allowed for snow to be falling across the area until it ended in the mid-afternoon. Before its conclusion, there was a time where a clearly defined band of snow stalled, and pivoted around a central location in southwest Virginia. This allowed for the axis of heaviest snowfall to be centered along the Interstate 77 corridor.||Snowfall totals averaged 6 to 10 inches across the northwest piedmont region, 8 to 12 inches across the northern mountains region, and 12 to 18 inches along the foothills, with the top end of this range along and near Interstate 77 where some 20 to 21 inch totals were experienced.||Sleet was confined to primarily Rockingham and Caswell counties were up to one inch was reported. Freezing rain occurred mainly over eastern Caswell county where up to two tenths of an inch occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 10 inches with the greatest amounts near the Yanceyville area.  There was a period of time during the overnight hours from the 12th into the 13th that the precipitation was in the form of sleet or freezing rain. As much as one inch of sleet fell across the county, especially in the Providence area. Freezing rain produced one to two tenths of an inch of ice across mainly the eastern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure formed along a nearly stationary frontal boundary over northern Florida on February 11th, with a strong and large area of high pressure to the north.  Precipitation began as snow from the I-95 corridor and points west, with mainly sleet and freezing rain reported east of I-95.  The northwest portion of the region received over five inches of snow. Over the course of a couple of days, the storm transitioned to mainly an ice event, with nearly an inch of ice reported over interior portions of northeast South Carolina, with lesser amounts over southeast North Carolina.  In addition to creating extremely hazardous driving conditions, major power outages were reported due to downed limbs and power lines.  Low pressure finally moved up the eastern seaboard and out of the region during the day on February 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain began falling the morning of February 11th, with a tenth of an inch of ice accumulation reported near the coast.  Driving was treacherous, with numerous power outages reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system affecting the Atlantic coast was followed by a strong arctic cold front passing across the region during the early morning hours of January 3rd, resulting in brief but heavy accumulating snow confined mainly to the west-facing slopes from southeast West Virginia through the mountains of North Carolina.  Winds with the arctic surge topped 60 mph in a few places across the mountains and, when combined with snow, resulted in periods of localized blizzard-like conditions.  Temperatures dropped into the teens and the positive single digits across the mountains, resulting flash-freezing of wet surfaces from rainfall on January 2nd.  Wind chills across the mountains were exceptionally cold, dropping into the negative teens.  Gradient winds began to relax by sunrise on the 3rd, allowing snow shower activity and blowing snow to diminish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down in the community of Deep Gap.  Another tree was blown down near Stony Point.

However, during the day on Thursday, February 13, 2014, the low pressure progressed northeast of the region, and sub-freezing air in the lower parts of the atmosphere returned and allowed for snow to be falling across the area until it ended in the mid-afternoon. Before its conclusion, there was a time where a clearly defined band of snow stalled, and pivoted around a central location in southwest Virginia. This allowed for the axis of heaviest snowfall to be centered along the Interstate 77 corridor.||Snowfall totals averaged 6 to 10 inches across the northwest piedmont region, 8 to 12 inches across the northern mountains region, and 12 to 18 inches along the foothills, with the top end of this range along and near Interstate 77 where some 20 to 21 inch totals were experienced.||Sleet was confined to primarily Rockingham and Caswell counties were up to one inch was reported. Freezing rain occurred mainly over eastern Caswell county where up to two tenths of an inch occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from around 7 inches in the southeast part of the county to close to 12 inches across the northwest part. Some sleet occurred during the overnight hours from the 12th into the 13th. Total sleet amounts were generally less than one-half inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure formed along a nearly stationary frontal boundary over northern Florida on February 11th, with a strong and large area of high pressure to the north.  Precipitation began as snow from the I-95 corridor and points west, with mainly sleet and freezing rain reported east of I-95.  The northwest portion of the region received over five inches of snow. Over the course of a couple of days, the storm transitioned to mainly an ice event, with nearly an inch of ice reported over interior portions of northeast South Carolina, with lesser amounts over southeast North Carolina.  In addition to creating extremely hazardous driving conditions, major power outages were reported due to downed limbs and power lines.  Low pressure finally moved up the eastern seaboard and out of the region during the day on February 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain began falling the morning of February 11th, with around a quarter inch of ice accumulation reported at Sunset Beach.  Driving was treacherous, with numerous power outages reported.

They were trying to heat their home with a charcoal grill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached eastern North Carolina during the afternoon of April 25th 2014. Warm southerly winds ahead of the front produced good instability over the region and numerous showers and thunderstorms crossed during the afternoon and evening. Two super-cell thunderstorms produced the bulk of the severe weather with multiple tornadoes reported in parts of Greene, Pitt and Beaufort counties. There was one indirect fatality as and elderly woman located near Washington died from pneumonia as a result of injuries sustained during the tornado. Total dollar damage was estimated at 15 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down approximately 1.5 miles west of Chocowinity along Taylor Road causing EF0 damage to trees and mobile homes. The tornado then strengthened to a strong EF2 as it impacted many homes and businesses from the intersection of U.S. Highway 17 and North Carolina Highway 33 eastward for about 1 mile. Several homes had significant damage in this area with numerous trees snapped.||The tornado reached EF3 intensity with winds estimated at 150 mph as it impacted homes along Whichards Beach Road from near Warrens Way to Harbor Drive. Many homes in this area were severely damaged or destroyed. Numerous vehicles and boats were damaged or destroyed.||The tornado then moved into the Washington Park area near Shorewood Drive and Daniels Drive after crossing the Pamlico River. Strong EF2 damage continued for about five miles as the tornado tracked eastward across U.S. Highway 264 to just north of the intersection of North Carolina Highway 32. Extensive damage occurred to numerous homes with some completely destroyed. The tornado continue to track east of North Carolina Highway 32 and north of U.S. Highway 264 for about 10 miles producing mainly EF0 to EF1 damage. The tornado finally dissipated near the Terra Ceia farms area.||There was one indirect fatality and 16 injuries associated with this tornado. It is estimated 150 to 200 homes and structures were damaged with many destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed during the afternoon within an unstable air mass across the western Piedmont. A few of the storms produced marginally severe hail and downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported a tree blown down on Old Beatty Ford Rd at Bev Rd, at Upper Palmer Rd and Gold Knob Rd, and on Yost Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unstable air was situated over the region south of a warm front and east of an approaching cold front. Showers and storms developed during the late afternoon and continued into the nighttime. A couple of storms increased to severe levels and produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck and damaged portions of a house along Highway 150 about 0.7 miles east of the intersection with Turner Road. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed over during the afternoon and evening of July 28th along the North Carolina coast. Several of the storms became severe producing strong winds and very large hail. Baseball size hail in Havelock damaged many vehicle and structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported nickel sized hail fell in Riverdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and an attendant cold front crossed the Mid-Atlantic Region on April 25, producing severe wind gusts and four tornadoes, including two long tracked EF-2 tornadoes over northeast North Carolina during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak EF-0 tornado touched down north of Elizabeth City near the intersection of Highway 17 and North St. The tornado tracked northeast removing shingles from homes and snapping numerous trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extensive QLCS developed ahead of a strong cold front. The QLCS raced eastward across the forecast area during the early to mid-morning hours on the 21st, exiting the coastal plain by the afternoon. The line produced scattered wind damage along the way, along with a brief EF-1 tornado touchdown near Grantham in Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree blown down along Walter Grissom Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extensive QLCS developed ahead of a strong cold front. The QLCS raced eastward across the forecast area during the early to mid-morning hours on the 21st, exiting the coastal plain by the afternoon. The line produced scattered wind damage along the way, along with a brief EF-1 tornado touchdown near Grantham in Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was blown down near Gillespie Street and Snow Hill Church Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms, with a few severe storms developed over the Piedmont late in the afternoon and during the early evening. Meanwhile, a weakening complex of thunderstorms moved into the Smoky Mountain region, producing areas of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extensive QLCS developed ahead of a strong cold front. The QLCS raced eastward across the forecast area during the early to mid-morning hours on the 21st, exiting the coastal plain by the afternoon. The line produced scattered wind damage along the way, along with a brief EF-1 tornado touchdown near Grantham in Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large pine tree was blown along with other large limbs along Ellis Road near NC Highway 147.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough shifted southeast out of the Ohio Valley and moved into a moderately unstable Southern Appalachian Region during the afternoon hours generating severe convection. Severe weather mode was almost entirely straight line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down near Hayesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous strong to severe thunderstorms moved into and developed over the North Carolina mountains during the evening ahead of an unusually strong mid-Summer cold front. Many of the storms exhibited characteristics more typical of organized storms seen in the Spring. While wind damage was the primary impact from the storms, a few instances of large hail were observed. One man was killed when a tree fell on his vehicle near Cherokee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reported lightning struck a barn near the intersection of antioch road and anthony drive, resulting in a significant structure fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms produced over five inches of rain with extensive flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A foot of standing water was reported on the streets of the Castle Bay Golf Course.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed during the afternoon, with several of those becoming strong to briefly severe, primarily across the foothills and Piedmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported golf ball size hail north of Shelby. Another spotter reported quarter size hail closer to town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Organized convection developed across central North Carolina in advance of a slow moving upper level trough stretching from the lower Ohio Valley south-southwestward into the lower Mississippi Valley. Locally heavily rainfall of two to three inches fell across the central Piedmont resulting in minor flooding across mostly urban areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: McCrimmon Parkway closed due to flooding between Church Street and NC Highway 54.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed over during the afternoon and evening of July 28th along the North Carolina coast. Several of the storms became severe producing strong winds and very large hail. Baseball size hail in Havelock damaged many vehicle and structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball to hen egg size hail reported outside of Cherry Point Marine Base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the northern mountains of North Carolina during the late afternoon and evening before moving into the foothills. A couple of storms briefly became strong to severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported nickel to quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the high terrain throughout the afternoon and moved east, while additional numerous thunderstorms moved into western North Carolina from East Tennessee during the late afternoon and early evening. Multiple localized severe thunderstorms developed from this activity and produced quite a bit of large hail along with isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media relayed county comms report of trees blown down on Wall Pool Rd and on Lytle Mountain Rd in the same area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extensive QLCS developed ahead of a strong cold front. The QLCS raced eastward across the forecast area during the early to mid-morning hours on the 21st, exiting the coastal plain by the afternoon. The line produced scattered wind damage along the way, along with a brief EF-1 tornado touchdown near Grantham in Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down in the Princeton Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm drifted south across northern Robeson County during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 211 was reportedly closed in both directions due to large trees down across the roadway near the intersection with Opal Road. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southward advancing surface trough along with a disturbance moving through northwesterly to west-northwesterly flow aloft combined with a warm and moist air mass in advance of the southward moving trough to produce and isolated severe storm across Harnett County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down on Darroch Road near Farrar Dairy Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across eastern North Carolina on July 15th as a cold front approached from the west. A few of the storms became severe producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds below down some trees along Highway 117 between Warsaw and Faison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With an upper level trough persisting to the west of the region, weak disturbances aloft within the southwesterly flow triggered convection given the moderately unstable and moist air mass. A few of the storms produced damaging wind gusts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon showers and thunderstorms developing across the mountains formed into a broken line that moved across the foothills and Piedmont during the late afternoon and evening. A few of the storms became briefly severe, producing localized downbursts. Locally heavy rainfall and urban flooding was reported in the Charlotte metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms reported numerous trees blown down on Gaston St near Tuckaseegee Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed over during the afternoon and evening of July 28th along the North Carolina coast. Several of the storms became severe producing strong winds and very large hail. Baseball size hail in Havelock damaged many vehicle and structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported two inch hail in Havelock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms associated with a frontal boundary produced heavy rain across portions of northeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated two inches of rain resulted in water over the roadway at North Road Street and West Broad Street in Elizabeth City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms associated with a cold front produced heavy rain across portions of northeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of rain in 35 minutes was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Arthur moving northward along the East Coast produced tropical storm force winds and locally heavy rainfall across portions of northeast North Carolina from late Thursday night, July 3rd into midday Friday, July 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain bands associated with Hurricane Arthur produced generally one to two inches of rainfall across portions of the county. Hertford (5.2 miles SE) reported 1.84 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Arthur moving northward along the East Coast produced tropical storm force winds and locally heavy rainfall across portions of northeast North Carolina from late Thursday night, July 3rd into midday Friday, July 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain bands associated with Hurricane Arthur produced generally one to two inches of rainfall across portions of the county. Wind gust of 37 knots was measured at Jarvisburg. Wind gust of 35 knots was measured at ONX.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Arthur moving northward along the East Coast produced tropical storm force winds and locally heavy rainfall across portions of northeast North Carolina from late Thursday night, July 3rd into midday Friday, July 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain bands associated with Hurricane Arthur produced generally one to two inches of rainfall across portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Arthur moving northward along the East Coast produced tropical storm force winds and locally heavy rainfall across portions of northeast North Carolina from late Thursday night, July 3rd into midday Friday, July 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain bands associated with Hurricane Arthur produced generally one to two inches of rainfall across portions of the county. Corolla (3.2 miles SSE) reported 2.35 inches of rain. Tropical storm wind gusts caused minor structural damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Arthur moving northward along the East Coast produced tropical storm force winds and locally heavy rainfall across portions of northeast North Carolina from late Thursday night, July 3rd into midday Friday, July 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain bands associated with Hurricane Arthur produced generally one to two inches of rainfall across portions of the county. Elizabeth City (ECG) reported 1.33 inches of rain. Elizabeth City (4 miles NW) reported 1.30 inches of rain. Wind gust of 45 knots was measured at ECG.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds and heavy rain which caused some minor flooding across portions of northeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed. Shingles were blown off a roof. Many large limbs were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds and heavy rain which caused some minor flooding across portions of northeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from scattered thunderstorms caused minor flooding over three areas of Highway 305.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds and heavy rain which caused some minor flooding across portions of northeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from scattered thunderstorms caused minor flooding over two areas of Early Station Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorm in advance of a cold front produced lightning damage across portions of northeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning strike caused a house fire in Corolla.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced heavy rain across portions of northeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter measured 3.50 inches of rain in northern Gates county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Arthur moving northward along the East Coast produced tropical storm force winds and locally heavy rainfall across portions of northeast North Carolina from late Thursday night, July 3rd into midday Friday, July 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain bands associated with Hurricane Arthur produced generally one to two inches of rainfall across portions of the county. Greens Cross reported 2.08 inches of rain. Windsor reported 1.86 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a frontal boundary approaching from the west and Hurricane Arthur moving northward along the North Carolina coast produced a brief weak tornado across portions of northeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF-0 tornado touched down near Saluda Road north of Ahoskie and tracked about one mile northwest to Hall Siding Road. At 203 Hall Siding Road, a house had its carport removed and an outbuilding destroyed. The house itself lost about 30 percent of its shingles. Significant damage to crops also occurred on Saluda Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Arthur moving northward along the East Coast produced tropical storm force winds and locally heavy rainfall across portions of northeast North Carolina from late Thursday night, July 3rd into midday Friday, July 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain bands associated with Hurricane Arthur produced generally one to two inches of rainfall across portions of the county. Edenton (0.2 mile NNW) reported 2.20 inches of rain. Wind gust of 36 knots was measured at EDE (Edenton).
